Title: Improved fruit α-tocopherol, carotenoid, squalene and phytosterol contents through manipulation of Brassica juncea 3-HYDROXY-3-METHYLGLUTARYL-COA SYNTHASE1 in transgenic tomato

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Overexpression of BjHMGS1 in tomato is a promising metabolic-engineering strategy to simultaneously elevate multiple health-promoting isoprenoids in edible fruit, including squalene, phytosterols, α-tocopherol, and carotenoids. The S359A hyperactive variant is preferable when the priority is stronger enhancement of MVA-derived squalene and phytosterols, whereas wild-type BjHMGS1 may be preferable when maximizing carotenoids/antioxidant activity. Deployment should consider pathway cross-talk, unequal effects on different end products, and possible flux redistribution between MVA- and MEP-derived branches.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Condenses acetoacetyl-CoA with acetyl-CoA to form 3-hydroxy-3-methylglutaryl-CoA (HMG-CoA) in the cytosolic mevalonate pathway.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Overexpression of Brassica juncea BjHMGS1 in tomato increased fruit contents of squalene, sterol intermediates, total phytosterols, α-tocopherol, lycopene, and β-carotene, and enhanced plant growth. The S359A mutant version produced stronger increases in squalene and phytosterols than wild-type BjHMGS1.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transgenic overexpression of wild-type and S359A BjHMGS1 in tomato; Western blot and RT-PCR verification; qRT-PCR of downstream pathway genes; GC-MS metabolite profiling of squalene, sterols, and α-tocopherol; HPLC carotenoid profiling; growth phenotyping; prior recombinant enzyme assay evidence cited for higher S359A activity.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Greenhouse/tissue-culture growth; fruits analyzed at 57 days after pollination (breaker +15); no special stress treatment reported.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Transgene expression was analyzed in 3-week-old seedlings, 5-week-old leaves, and 57-DAP mature fruits; metabolic effects were prominent in mature fruits. The paper also notes endogenous tomato SlHMGR1 is associated with early fruit development, whereas SlHMGR2 is associated with maturation/ripening.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The study tested both wild-type BjHMGS1 and a hyperactive S359A mutant previously shown to have ~10-fold higher in vitro activity. BjHMGS1 overexpression induced broad transcriptional changes in both MVA- and MEP-related genes, supporting cross-talk between the two pathways. Increased MVA-derived products in OE-S359A were attributed to heightened expression of SlHMGR2, SlFPS1, SlSQS, and SlCYP710A11 in fruits. However, S359A did not outperform wild type for α-tocopherol and produced lower lycopene and total carotenoids than wild-type BjHMGS1.
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Core upstream MVA precursor-supply module feeding sterol, squalene, brassinosteroid, dolichol, and cross-talk-linked plastidial isoprenoid branches
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: No obvious growth penalty was reported; instead, growth was enhanced. However, flux redistribution was observed: the S359A variant, despite stronger enhancement of squalene/phytosterols, showed lower lycopene and total carotenoids than wild-type BjHMGS1, indicating branch-specific tradeoffs in product accumulation.
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: HMGS / condensing enzyme of the mevalonate pathway
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key upstream control point / not directly established as the sole rate-limiting step in this paper
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Overexpression of Brassica juncea BjHMGS1 in tomato increased MVA-derived squalene, sterol intermediates, and total phytosterols in fruit. 2. BjHMGS1 overexpression also elevated MEP-derived α-tocopherol and carotenoids, accompanied by induction of plastidial SlGPS and SlGGPPS1, indicating MVA–MEP pathway cross-talk. 3. The S359A hyperactive BjHMGS1 variant produced stronger increases in squalene and phytosterols than wild-type BjHMGS1, but did not improve α-tocopherol further and showed lower lycopene/total carotenoids than wild-type.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Class: Isoprenoid / triterpenoid sterol; vitamin E; carotenoid
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Squalene and phytosterols are health-promoting dietary isoprenoids; phytosterols can lower blood cholesterol. α-Tocopherol and carotenoids function as antioxidants, and elevated carotenoids increased fruit radical-scavenging activity.
